async_execution.mechanism: hook_execution_mode: Codex supports async hook execution mode for fire-and-forget background hook runs

context_injection.mechanism: hook_system_message: Codex hooks can inject a system message into the agent's active session context

decision_control.mechanism: hook_result_abort: Codex hooks can abort (block) the triggering action; allow and modify sub-capabilities documented via hook result schema

handler_types.mechanism: hook_handler_types: Codex supports shell command and LLM prompt handler types; TypeScript extension type also documented

hook_scopes.mechanism: hook_scope: Codex hooks can be scoped to global/user or project configuration

input_modification.mechanism: hook_updated_input: Codex PreToolUse hooks return modified input arguments that replace the original tool input

json_io_protocol.mechanism: Codex hooks communicate via exit codes and stdout text; no structured JSON stdin/stdout protocol documented

matcher_patterns.mechanism: hook_matcher: Codex hooks support pattern matching to filter which tools or events trigger the hook

permission_control.mechanism: hook_permission_decision: Codex hooks can return permission decisions that grant or deny tool availability

providerExtensions.name: Hook Events
providerExtensions.description: Codex supports five hook events: PreToolUse, PostToolUse, SessionStart, UserPromptSubmit, and Stop. Event names use PascalCase in the config.toml hooks file (under [hooks.PreToolUse], etc.) and camelCase in the TypeScript app-server protocol (preToolUse, postToolUse, sessionStart, userPromptSubmit, stop). In the Rust wire payload, event names serialize as PascalCase strings (HookEventNameWire enum).

providerExtensions.name: Hook Handler Types
providerExtensions.description: Each hook entry specifies a handler type via the type field. Three types are supported: command (runs a shell command), prompt (invokes a prompt), and agent (delegates to a sub-agent). The command type is the primary type for external scripts. Command handlers support: command (required string), timeout/timeoutSec (optional duration in seconds), async (bool, default false — runs hook asynchronously without blocking the operation), and statusMessage (optional display string).

providerExtensions.name: Tool Matcher (PreToolUse / PostToolUse)
providerExtensions.description: PreToolUse and PostToolUse hook entries can include an optional matcher field (a string pattern) that filters which tool calls trigger the hook. When absent, the hook fires for all tool calls. This allows attaching hooks to specific tools by name.

providerExtensions.name: Hook Execution Mode (sync / async)
providerExtensions.description: Hook handlers can run synchronously (blocking the current operation until the hook completes) or asynchronously (fire-and-forget, does not block). Exposed in the TypeScript protocol as HookExecutionMode with values sync and async. In the Rust config, the async field on HookHandlerConfig::Command controls this for command handlers.

providerExtensions.name: Hook Scope (thread / turn)
providerExtensions.description: Hooks can be scoped to a thread (lifetime of the entire session) or a turn (single request/response cycle). Exposed in the TypeScript app-server protocol as HookScope with values thread and turn.

providerExtensions.name: Hook Result Abort vs Continue
providerExtensions.description: The Rust hook engine distinguishes three outcomes: Success, FailedContinue (hook failed but subsequent hooks and the operation continue), and FailedAbort (hook failed and the operation is aborted, no further hooks run). This tri-state is more expressive than a simple pass/fail. For command hooks, the JSON output schema supports a continue boolean (default true) and a decision field (approve/block for PreToolUse, block for PostToolUse) to control operation flow.

providerExtensions.name: Suppress Output Flag
providerExtensions.description: Hook command output schemas include a suppressOutput boolean (default false). When true, the hook's stdout/stderr is not surfaced to the user. Available on all five event output schemas.

providerExtensions.name: System Message Injection
providerExtensions.description: Hook command output schemas include a systemMessage field. When set, the hook can inject additional context into the system message for the current turn. Available on all five event output schemas.

providerExtensions.name: Turn ID in Hook Payload
providerExtensions.description: The hook input schemas (pre-tool-use, post-tool-use) include a turn_id field marked as a Codex extension. This exposes the active turn ID to internal turn-scoped hooks, allowing hooks to correlate their actions with specific model turns.

providerExtensions.name: Updated Tool Input (PreToolUse)
providerExtensions.description: The PreToolUse hook output schema includes an updatedInput field. When set by the hook, the tool's input is replaced with the hook-provided value before the tool executes. This allows hooks to modify or sanitize tool arguments before execution.

providerExtensions.name: Permission Decision (PreToolUse)
providerExtensions.description: The PreToolUse hook output schema includes a permissionDecision field (allow, deny, ask) within hookSpecificOutput. This gives hooks the ability to make fine-grained permission decisions about tool execution, separate from the top-level approve/block decision.
